items.q: Was the 1923 Munich Putsch a complete failure for Hitler?
items.correct: Militarily yes — it collapsed in hours — but the trial gave Hitler a national platform and he wrote Mein Kampf in prison, learning to pursue power legally instead.
items.misconception: The Munich Putsch destroyed the Nazi Party until Hitler rebuilt it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: The Munich Putsch succeeded in overthrowing the Bavarian government for several weeks.
items.why: The Nazis were small but the putsch was a propaganda gain. Students treat it as straightforward failure; in fact the lessons learned shaped the legal route to 1933.

items.correct: A combination of moral campaigning, slave resistance and rebellions (especially Haiti and Jamaica), and the shifting economic interest of industrial capitalism.
items.misconception: Wilberforce single-handedly persuaded Parliament to end slavery out of pure morality.
items.random_wrong: Slavery was abolished because cotton was no longer profitable.
items.why: Students inherit the heroic Wilberforce narrative. Recent scholarship emphasises slave revolts (Haiti, Demerara, Jamaica) and economic shifts — abolition was not a gift from above.

items.q: How did the Bolsheviks seize power in October 1917?
items.correct: Trotsky's Military Revolutionary Committee coordinated a near-bloodless takeover of key buildings in Petrograd from a Provisional Government that had lost army and worker support.
items.misconception: The October Revolution was a mass popular uprising that stormed the Winter Palace.
items.random_wrong: The October Revolution overthrew the Tsar.
items.why: It was a coordinated coup with limited fighting; the Tsar had been gone since February. Soviet propaganda mythologised it as a mass storming, which film cemented.
Correct answer: Bad air that spread disease. “Holy water” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Whipped themselves. “Sang hymns only” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many 'humours' did medieval doctors think the body had?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
